participants.name: Wei (d2ddej)
participants.professional_persona: Wei Jie is a postdoctoral fellow at Nanyang Technological University working on generative AI for drug discovery. He moved to Singapore from China after his PhD.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep knowledge in generative adversarial networks, variational autoencoders, and molecular graph generation. He holds a PhD in bioinformatics.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To translate his research into a biotech startup and contribute to Singapore's precision medicine initiatives.

participants.name: Asha
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-brqijob6
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-brqijob6
participants.professional_persona: Asha is a public policy analyst at a think tank that studies AI governance and social impact. She regularly contributes to national consultations on responsible AI.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in regulatory analysis, stakeholder engagement, and impact assessment; skilled in bridging technical AI concepts with policy recommendations.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: She hopes to shape Singapore's AI for Public Good framework and later work with international bodies on AI ethics standards.

participants.name: An (hq6t9a)
participants.professional_persona: An experienced AI consultant who left a global tech firm to join a boutique agency that helps traditional enterprises adopt AI. He enjoys designing proof-of-concepts that demonstrate quick ROI.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Versatile in cloud AI services (Azure, GCP), building recommendation systems, and conducting AI readiness assessments using the AISG AIRI framework. Adept at managing client relationships.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Plans to write a practical guide on AI adoption for SMEs and create a community of practice for AI practitioners across different sectors.

participants.name: Heng
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-faigxb1m
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-faigxb1m
participants.professional_persona: Heng is an AI safety engineer at a multinational corporation's Singapore R&D centre. He specialises in stress-testing AI systems to ensure robustness against adversarial attacks.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep knowledge of adversarial machine learning, verification methods, and red-teaming; experienced in implementing Responsible AI principles in production.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: He aims to establish a regional centre of excellence for AI safety and contribute to Singapore's trusted AI ecosystem.

participants.name: A (fys7i5)
participants.professional_persona: A mid-level AI practitioner who previously worked in e-commerce and now develops fraud detection models for a digital bank. He is passionate about responsible AI and model fairness.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Competent in anomaly detection, graph neural networks, and bias mitigation. Uses tools like SHAP and Fairlearn to audit model decisions.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to specialise in AI ethics and governance, and eventually help shape Singapore's guidelines on trustworthy AI deployment.

initial_posts.narrative: As a policy analyst focused on AI for public good, I interpret the resource logic as stating over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D, which I take as the total funding for the NAIS 2.0 period. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is explicitly stated. For compute, given the emphasis on infrastructure and the need to support 100E projects and other initiatives, I estimate 5 million GPU hours as a reasonable allocation for the 2023 window, considering typical cloud compute grants. For trainers, based on the AI Apprenticeship Programme and upskilling goals, I estimate 200 personnel, reflecting a modest team to guide enterprises and practitioners.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million total funding and 200 trainers, I estimate that AI Singapore will conduct about 80 enterprise outreach sessions in 2023 to engage firms for problem statement submission under the 100E programme. This is based on a typical pace of 1-2 sessions per week, targeting diverse sectors. For researcher matchmaking, with 20 events, I assume a bi-weekly cadence to pair researchers with enterprise challenges, leveraging the trainer pool and compute resources. Training workshops are estimated at 100, reflecting the need to upskill 200 trainers and reach enterprises, aligning with the 100E goal. Platform onboarding assists are pegged at 150, assuming each of the 100E projects requires 1-2 sessions, plus additional support for other initiatives. These estimates are conservative but grounded in the resource logic and my policy analysis perspective, emphasizing efficient use of public funds for public good.
